title: Slow Clients, Resource Limits, Backpressure, Admission, and Cleanup · Go Semantics Case: Write Backpressure
summary: A server gives every connection a goroutine and unlimited buffer, refreshes deadlines on single-byte trickles, queues output without bounds, and retries accept failures until the process collapses. Planned artifact: Add and verify the write backpressure increment in connection resource budget and overload policy. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Propagate slow peer writes through bounded queues, cancellation, deadlines, partial output evidence, and producer shutdown.
Bound per-connection buffers, retained slices, field maps, content queues, trailer data, and pooled-object capacity before allocation.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Predict Write Backpressure · Theory Write Backpressure
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory protocol team handles case 5443 in a partner TLS endpoint negotiating HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2 through ALPN. Build deterministic Go evidence to combine absolute phase time, memory, and output-queue budgets for slow-peer defense; preserve this operating constraint: the maintained Go protocol stack remains the production transfer target; then append a second pipelined message and prove the first parser leaves the exact residual bytes. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Propagate slow peer writes through bounded queues, cancellation, deadlines, partial output evidence, and producer shutdown.

steps.title: Build Write Backpressure evidence · Theory Write Backpressure
steps.instruction:
Append a compile-ready pure-Go function headed "// Evidence: httpproto-write-backpressure-14dlso" that uses case 5416 to combine absolute phase time, memory, and output-queue budgets for slow-peer defense. Browser code must not open sockets or listeners, negotiate TLS, contact networks or proxies, capture packets, read host state, or use credentials; verify those boundaries later with the named Go 1.26 protocol transfer gates.
